stories.headline: Sheridan teachers' strike ends after tentative agreement reached, educators to return Monday
stories.summary:
Sheridan educators could return to classrooms as soon as Monday after the district and striking teachers reached a tentative agreement, Gov. Jared Polis's office announced Thursday evening. The strike had lasted the entirety of April, making it one of the lengthier teacher work stoppages in recent Colorado history.

Details of the tentative agreement were not immediately disclosed. The governor's office played a role in facilitating the resolution between the district and its educators.

The Sheridan School District serves a small, predominantly low-income community south of Denver. The end of the strike, if ratified, would allow students to return to in-person instruction after weeks of disruption.

stories.headline: Wildfires erupt near Horsetooth Reservoir and south of Colorado Springs; firefighter injured, evacuations lifted
stories.summary:
A wildfire near Horsetooth Reservoir west of Fort Collins prompted mandatory evacuations Thursday, and a firefighter was injured battling the blaze. By Thursday evening, the Poudre Fire Authority announced the fire had been contained and all evacuation orders lifted, with crews remaining on scene to extinguish hotspots.

South of Colorado Springs, El Paso County officials lifted evacuation orders for the Hammer fire near the unincorporated community of Hanover after crews achieved 60 percent containment. That fire had been burning since Wednesday.

The outbreaks come amid an extreme fire weather period across Colorado. Parts of southern Colorado were placed under a Particularly Dangerous Situation Red Flag Warning — a designation that had never previously been issued for the area.

stories.headline: Red Flag Warning returns to Front Range and Eastern Plains Friday as wind and low humidity persist
stories.summary:
A Red Flag Warning will go back into effect for the Front Range and Eastern Plains from 11 a.m. to 8 p.m. Friday, with wind gusts up to 40 mph and humidity values as low as 10 percent, according to Denver7 meteorologists. Daytime highs are expected to reach the low to mid 70s along the Interstate 25 corridor.

Relief is forecast for the weekend, with scattered showers expected Saturday and a better chance of rain and possibly thunderstorms Sunday. Cooler, unsettled weather is expected to persist into early next week before more rain returns mid-week.

The fire danger has been elevated across Colorado for much of April, with 2026 already setting records for Red Flag Warning days. Residents along the Front Range and foothills are urged to avoid any activity that could spark a fire through the end of the week.

stories.headline: Denver man sentenced to 48 years in prison for 2022 triple shooting that killed two teenagers and a truck driver
stories.summary:
Luis Rivas-Newcomb, 20, was sentenced to 48 years in prison after pleading guilty to second-degree murder in a 2022 shooting near 38th Avenue and Peoria Street in east Denver that killed three people. Rivas-Newcomb was 15 years old at the time of the shooting and was initially charged with three counts of first-degree murder.

Investigators said two sedans were traveling south on Peoria when Rivas-Newcomb fired into the other vehicle, causing it to crash into oncoming traffic and strike a pickup truck. The shooting killed 13-year-old Jayden Hoyle and 14-year-old Adrion Foster at the scene, and truck driver Uriel Reyes Medina, 37, died at a hospital.

Denver District Attorney John Walsh called the shooting horrifying and said the sentence makes Denver's streets safer. An attorney for Rivas-Newcomb declined to comment.

stories.headline: Former Adams and Broomfield CASA director settles $99,000 fraud case with Colorado Attorney General
stories.summary:
The former executive director of CASA of Adams and Broomfield Counties falsified invoices to pocket nearly $100,000 in nonprofit donations intended to fund her graduate school tuition, the Colorado Attorney General's Office announced. Lindsay Salas enrolled at the University of Denver for an MBA program in 2022 and began submitting altered and fabricated invoices to the nonprofit, collecting reimbursements from DU that should have gone back to CASA.

Salas and the attorney general's office filed a civil settlement agreement Wednesday. Under the agreement, Salas will repay $66,000 to the state over six years — funds the state will return to CASA — and is barred from serving in financial or fundraising roles for charitable organizations for five years.

The settlement resolves the civil claims but does not preclude additional legal action, state officials said. CASA organizations coordinate court-appointed special advocates who represent children in the legal system who have experienced abuse or neglect. An attorney for Salas did not respond to a request for comment.

stories.headline: Harassment charge against Boulder police officer dismissed after December domestic dispute arrest
stories.summary:
The First Judicial District Attorney's Office has dismissed a harassment charge against Boulder Police Department officer Jonathan Hill, 34, who was arrested in December following a reported domestic dispute. Arvada police arrested Hill after a woman said he picked her up and moved her away from his pickup truck during a child custody exchange, according to an arrest affidavit.

The DA's Office dismissed the charge Friday, and the case has been sealed, preventing officials from explaining why. Hill, who joined the Boulder Police Department in April 2024, remains employed while an internal professional standards administrative investigation is ongoing.

Hill told police he physically moved the woman because he did not trust her near his property. The Boulder Police Department did not say whether Hill remains on paid administrative leave following the charge's dismissal.

stories.headline: Beverage distributor plans to lay off 320 Littleton-area employees as Reyes Holdings acquires its Colorado assets
stories.summary:
Republic National Distributing Company plans to lay off approximately 320 Denver-area employees at its Littleton facility as part of a consolidation deal in which Reyes Holdings is acquiring its Colorado assets. The move marks a significant reduction in the local workforce for one of the region's major alcohol beverage distributors.

The acquisition by Reyes Holdings, one of the largest beer and beverage distributors in the United States, continues a broader trend of consolidation in the wholesale beverage distribution industry. Details on the timeline of the layoffs and asset transfer were not immediately available.

The cuts add to a string of notable layoffs in the Denver metro area in recent months, including PNC Bank's announced reduction of 777 employees at its Lakewood facility following the FirstBank merger.

stories.headline: Legal software firm Litera opens downtown Denver tech office near Union Station, plans to hire 20 engineers
stories.summary:
Litera, a legal software company, has opened a 3,872-square-foot office in downtown Denver near Union Station, the company announced. The new space is focused on artificial intelligence development and represents the company's expanded presence in the Colorado tech market.

The company plans to hire 20 additional engineers at the Denver location. Litera provides software tools for law firms and legal departments, and the Denver office is intended to serve as a hub for its growing AI product development efforts.

The opening adds to a string of technology companies establishing or expanding offices in downtown Denver, particularly in the Union Station neighborhood, which has become one of the region's more active commercial real estate submarkets.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers propose bill to let parole board release prisoners if Polis fails to act on rehabilitation program graduates
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators have introduced Senate Bill 26-158, which would allow the State Parole Board to approve early releases for graduates of a specialized rehabilitation program for people who committed crimes as juveniles or young adults, if the governor does not act on an application within 60 days. The bill is a direct response to Gov. Jared Polis halting all releases from the Juveniles and Young Adults Convicted as Adults Program, known as JYACAP, since 2023.

Polis approved the release of all 17 program graduates between 2020 and 2023, but has since stalled the program, leaving about a dozen prisoners who completed the three-year curriculum waiting behind bars. Polis has cited discomfort with the governor's sole authority over the release decision.

The bill, sponsored by Sen. Mike Weissman, an Aurora Democrat, would apply to the existing backlog of waiting prisoners as well as future program graduates. It also adds a new curriculum requirement for prisoners to acknowledge the impact and trauma of their crimes. JYACAP was established in 2016 following a U.S. Supreme Court ruling that children are constitutionally different from adults and cannot be automatically sentenced to life without parole.

stories.headline: Nuggets lose Game 3 to Timberwolves with Aaron Gordon sidelined by calf injury
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets entered Game 3 of their first-round playoff series in Minneapolis without forward Aaron Gordon, who was ruled out Thursday night with left calf tightness. Gordon had appeared on the injury report as probable, then questionable, before head coach David Adelman ruled him out shortly before tipoff at Target Center.

Gordon, 30, began feeling tightness after Game 2 on Monday. With the series already tied 1-1, his absence put the Nuggets in a difficult position. Undrafted wing Spencer Jones, 24, started in Gordon's place and drew the assignment of guarding Minnesota's Julius Randle.

Adelman did not rule out Gordon's return for Game 4 on Saturday but said he was uncertain how Gordon would respond to treatment. Gordon has dealt with recurring calf and hamstring issues throughout his career, including a right calf strain last season that he played through during the playoffs.

stories.headline: Broncos enter NFL Draft Days 2 and 3 with seven picks, trade activity expected
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos hold seven picks heading into the second and third days of the 2026 NFL Draft, with their first selection at No. 62 in the second round. General manager George Paton and head coach Sean Payton have a history of trading picks aggressively — last year alone they engineered four trades involving 20 draft picks — and further movement is widely expected this weekend.

Denver lacks a first-round pick this year, and the Broncos' brass has discussed whether to trade up for targeted players or collect additional capital by moving back. Payton has noted the roster is in strong enough shape that they can afford to be aggressive in moving up for a specific player if warranted.

The 2026 draft class is considered deep at inside linebacker, safety, and edge rusher, with thinner options at tight end and running back beyond the top tier. Paton and Payton have said their differing philosophies — Paton as a pick collector, Payton more aggressive about trading up — have melded well during their time together in Denver.

stories.headline: Greater roadrunner spotted near Parker Road in Denver metro, a rare sighting this far north
stories.summary:
A greater roadrunner was reportedly spotted last week near Parker Road and Fairmount Cemetery on the border of Denver and Arapahoe counties, a highly unusual sighting for an urbanized area well north of the bird's typical Colorado habitat, according to Colorado Parks and Wildlife. The sighting was logged on eBird on April 15.

CPW avian program manager Jim Gammonley said greater roadrunners are common in southeastern Colorado but extremely rare near the Denver metro area. Over the past 15 years, only a small handful have been documented this far north. The dry, warm conditions across the West in recent months may be pushing some birds to explore areas outside their normal range.

Roadrunners typically inhabit open deserts with scrubby vegetation and can run at speeds exceeding 15 mph. The bird spotted near Denver is likely a greater roadrunner, which is native to the southwestern United States.

weather.notable: About 11 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I love the word 'checkmate.' It's got that confident feel. You never hear someone say, 'Let me just double-checkmate.'

weekendEvents.title: Alesso at Red Rocks
weekendEvents.description: Electronic music artist Alesso returns to Red Rocks Amphitheatre for a second night of performances.

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Mammoth vs. San Diego Seals (NLL Playoff)
weekendEvents.description: The Colorado Mammoth host the San Diego Seals in a win-or-go-home National Lacrosse League playoff game.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Summit FC vs. San Diego Wave FC
weekendEvents.description: Denver's professional women's soccer team plays their first home match at Dick's Sporting Goods Park against San Diego Wave FC.
weekendEvents.location: Dick's Sporting Goods Park, Commerce City

weekendEvents.title: Grace Helbig – 'Let Me Get This Off My Chest'
weekendEvents.description: Comedian and internet personality Grace Helbig brings her stand-up tour to Summit Music Hall.

weekendEvents.title: Come From Away (Touring)
weekendEvents.description: The Tony Award-winning musical about the small town of Gander, Newfoundland, and the 38 planes that landed there following the September 11 attacks plays a matinee at the Arvada Center.

weekendEvents.title: Hell's Kitchen (Musical)
weekendEvents.description: This new musical from Alicia Keys follows 17-year-old Ali in a coming-of-age New York City story featuring Keys' music. The production closes April 26.
weekendEvents.location: Denver Center for the Performing Arts, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Spring Mayfest at Marjorie Park
weekendEvents.description: A medieval spring celebration at the Museum of Outdoor Arts' Marjorie Park featuring flower crown weaving, Maypole folk dancing, live music, and an art-themed scavenger hunt. Each adult ticket includes three cocktails or bar/concession items.
weekendEvents.location: Museum of Outdoor Arts, Marjorie Park, Englewood

weekendEvents.title: Women's Film Festival (DenverFilm)
weekendEvents.description: A film festival hosted by DenverFilm showcasing movies made by women, including documentaries and narrative features across multiple screenings.

weekendEvents.title: Estes Park Bigfoot Days
weekendEvents.description: Annual festival in Bond Park featuring appearances from television Bigfoot celebrities and experts, live music, family activities, craft and food vendors, a half marathon and 5K, and a Bigfoot calling contest.

weekendEvents.title: For the Love of Pickles Pickle Fest
weekendEvents.description: Loveland Aleworks hosts this pickle-themed festival with pickle-inspired craft beers, live music, pickle-themed merchandise, and vendors. Free admission; beer tickets available for purchase.

weekendEvents.title: Denver's First Late Night Talk Show at RISE Comedy
weekendEvents.description: The first-ever late night talk show format in Denver makes its debut at RISE Comedy.

weekendEvents.title: Motorless Morning at Garden of the Gods
weekendEvents.description: Garden of the Gods closes all roads to motor vehicles for a quiet morning allowing visitors to walk, bike, and experience the park without traffic. Free admission.
weekendEvents.location: Garden of the Gods, Colorado Springs

topStory.headline: Iran War Has Depleted U.S. Missile Stockpiles, Strained Global Supply Chains, Officials Say
topStory.summary:
The United States military burned through critical weapons stockpiles during 38 days of combat operations against Iran, firing more than 1,000 Tomahawk cruise missiles — roughly 10 times the annual U.S. purchase rate — along with more than 1,200 Patriot interceptor missiles at over $4 million each, according to internal Pentagon estimates and congressional officials. The conflict, which ended with a ceasefire approximately two weeks ago, cost an estimated $28 billion to $35 billion, or nearly $1 billion per day, with $5.6 billion in munitions expended in the first two days alone.

The drawdowns have left U.S. military commands in Asia and Europe significantly less prepared to confront adversaries such as Russia and China, as the Pentagon rushed munitions from those theaters to the Middle East to sustain operations. Senator Jack Reed of Rhode Island, the ranking Democrat on the Senate Armed Services Committee, warned this week that restoring stockpiles to pre-war levels could take years at current production rates. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt disputed the core findings, stating that "the entire premise of this story is false."

Defense analysts say the conflict has exposed a deeper structural problem: the U.S. military's overreliance on costly precision munitions and air-defense interceptors, and the defense industry's limited capacity to scale production rapidly. Mark Cancian, a retired Marine Corps colonel and senior adviser at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, noted that several critical ground-attack and missile-defense munitions were already in short supply before the war began and are now in an even more precarious state.

Beyond the military dimension, the war has disrupted global supply chains with broad economic consequences. Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas passes, has driven up energy costs and cascading price increases in pharmaceuticals and other goods. Pharmacies in the United Kingdom are charging 20 to 30 percent more for over-the-counter medicines, while chemists in India report paracetamol prices rising by as much as 96 percent, driven by disrupted petrochemical feedstocks and strained air and sea logistics routes through the Persian Gulf region.

Experts warn that approximately 35 percent of pharmaceuticals globally move by air, and up to 90 percent of critical or life-saving medicines and vaccines do so as well. Wouter Dewulf, a logistics professor at the University of Antwerp, estimated that 22 percent of global air cargo flows are exposed to Middle East disruptions, with the primary near-term effects being delays, rerouting and higher costs rather than outright shortages — though further price increases remain likely if disruptions persist.
